content.body: Combinatorics is the mathematics of counting. How many ways can a race be won? How many passwords are possible? How many ways can you arrange a team? These questions use counting principles that are essential in probability, statistics, and computing.

Fundamental Counting Principle:
If event A can occur in m ways AND event B can occur in n ways,
then both events together can occur in m × n ways.

Example: 3 shirts and 4 trousers → 3×4=12 different outfits

Factorial: n! = n × (n-1) × (n-2) × ... × 2 × 1
5! = 5×4×3×2×1 = 120
0! = 1 (by definition)

PERMUTATIONS (ORDER MATTERS):
nPr = n! / (n-r)!

Number of ways to arrange r items chosen from n distinct items

Example: How many ways can 3 people be chosen as 1st, 2nd, 3rd from 8?
⁸P₃ = 8! / (8-3)! = 8!/5! = 8×7×6 = 336

content.title: Permutation Examples
content.body:
How many 3-digit numbers can be made from {1,2,3,4,5} without repeating?
= ⁵P₃ = 5!/2! = 5×4×3 = 60

How many ways can all 6 students sit in a row?
= 6! = 720

How many ways to arrange the letters in MATHS?
= 5! = 120 (all different letters)

Note: if letters repeat, divide by the factorial of each repeated letter

questions.question: 4 tops × 3 bottoms × 2 shoes = how many outfits?
How many ways can 5 people sit in a row?
⁸P₁ = ?
questions.explanation: ⁸P₁ = 8!/(8-1)! = 8!/7! = 8. Choosing 1 from 8 in order = 8 ways.

questions.question: 3-digit numbers from {1,2,3,4} no repeats: how many?
Gold, silver, bronze from 10 athletes: how many ways?
nP1 always equals:
questions.explanation: nP1 = n!/(n-1)! = n. Selecting 1 item from n in order = n choices.

content.body:
COMBINATIONS (ORDER DOES NOT MATTER):
nCr = n! / [r!(n-r)!]  also written C(n,r) or (n choose r)

When selecting a group where the order doesn't matter
(e.g. choosing a committee — same people in any order = same committee)

Key relationship: nCr = nC(n-r)
⁸C₃ = ⁸C₅ = 56

content.title: Combination Examples
content.body:
A class of 10: choose a committee of 4. How many ways?
¹⁰C₄ = 10!/(4!×6!) = (10×9×8×7)/(4×3×2×1) = 5040/24 = 210

Pick 3 fruits from {apple, banana, mango, pear, orange}: how many groups?
⁵C₃ = 10

Key: 'committee', 'team', 'group', 'select', 'choose' → COMBINATIONS
     'arrange', 'order', '1st/2nd/3rd', 'rank', 'password' → PERMUTATIONS

Pascal's Triangle:
Row 0:    1
Row 1:   1  1
Row 2:  1  2  1
Row 3: 1  3  3  1
Row 4:1  4  6  4  1

Each number = sum of two above it
Row n gives the coefficients of (a+b)ⁿ

Binomial Theorem:
(a+b)ⁿ = Σ nCr × a^(n-r) × bʳ

(a+b)² = a² + 2ab + b²
(a+b)³ = a³ + 3a²b + 3ab² + b³
(a+b)⁴ = a⁴ + 4a³b + 6a²b² + 4ab³ + b⁴

content.title: Binomial Expansion
content.body:
Expand (x+2)³:
= x³ + 3(x²)(2) + 3(x)(2²) + 2³
= x³ + 6x² + 12x + 8

Expand (2x-1)⁴:
Coefficients from row 4: 1, 4, 6, 4, 1
= (2x)⁴ + 4(2x)³(-1) + 6(2x)²(-1)² + 4(2x)(-1)³ + (-1)⁴
= 16x⁴ - 32x³ + 24x² - 8x + 1

questions.question: Choosing a group of 3 from 7: how many ways?
Which involves combinations (not permutations)?
questions.explanation: Team selection: order doesn't matter (same players = same team regardless of selection order). Use combinations.

questions.question: nCn = ?
questions.explanation: nCn = n!/(n!×0!) = 1/1 = 1. Only one way to choose all n items.

questions.question: Coefficient of x² in (1+x)⁴:
questions.explanation: Row 4 of Pascal: 1,4,6,4,1. Coefficient of x² (r=2) is ⁴C₂ = 6.

questions.question: In a lottery, choose 6 from 49 numbers. This uses:
questions.explanation: The same 6 numbers win regardless of the order you pick them — combinations.

questions.question: ⁶C₀ = ?
questions.explanation: nC0 = 1. There is exactly one way to choose nothing.
